original_abstract:
This article provides a practical-theological reflection on the necessity of equipping and mentoring children for service (*diakonia*) as a fundamental pathway to emulating the attitude of Christ. The central hypothesis is that children and youth are inherent, vital members of the church body who have received spiritual gifts from God for the purpose of edification. Consequently, the church and parents bear a significant responsibility to guide children toward fulfilling their calling as obedient followers of Jesus Christ.

The research is structured into four distinct phases following a reflective equilibrium methodology. The first phase offers a descriptive overview of current practical-theological research regarding youth ministry. The second phase engages in an intra-disciplinary dialogue with social and developmental psychology to analyze how inadequate mentoring and negative social attitudes impact the spiritual formation of children. Phase three establishes a normative framework by exploring basic-theoretical perspectives from Scripture, particularly focusing on the "diakon" word group. This analysis argues that *diakonia* is not merely humble service but a representative ministry of God’s grace and a key component of a believer's identity and spiritual maturity. 

The final phase offers practical-theoretical perspectives, highlighting that children who are actively engaged in service and obedience to Christ are better positioned to reflect His character to the world. The authors conclude that by intentionally mentoring children in their unique *diakonia*, parents and the faith community facilitate spiritual growth and ensure that children are not merely appendages to the church, but active participants in the *Missio Dei*.
humanized_abstract:
Die Gabe, zum Dienst reifen zu können. Kinder- und Jugenddiakonie im praktisch-theologischen Diskurs Die Frage nach der Diakonie des Kindes ist ein weit über das Religionswissenschaftliche hinausreichendes Problem. In diesem Artikel soll deshalb exemplarisch einer praktisch-theologischen Diskussion der Frage nachgegangen werden, in welcher Weise das Kind in die Diakonie evangelikal ausgerichteter Gemeinschaften einbezogen werden muß, um ihm die Möglichkeit zu eröffnen, zu Christo zum Vorbild zu gelangen. Hypothetisch unterstellt wird, dass Kindeskunde bzw. Kinderdiakonie/die Diakonie des Kindes keine bloße Fata morgana, sondern vielmehr ist es so, dass Kinder und Jugendliche als Mitglieder der Kirche Gottgeschenne sind, deren Aufgabe es ist, sie auszubilden und zu bestimmen, wie sie in Jesus Christus, dem Lehrer ihres Lebens, dienstbar zu sein haben.

Title Title Page - Title page Abstract Chapter 1: Research Design and Procedure for a Constructivist Understanding of Professional Ministry to Young People Chapter 1: Research Design and Procedure for a Constructivist Understanding of Professional Ministry to Young People The research was structured into four phases in accordance with a reflective equilibrium research strategy. In phase 1 it described current practical-theological research concerning professional youth ministry. The corresponding intra-disciplinary relationship was explored by means of a dialogue with social and developmental psychology regarding the implications for children’s spiritual development in the event of deficient mentoring and negative social attitudes towards children and youth ministry (phase 2). For phase 3, the basic-theoretical aspect was explored more specifically using scripture. This relates particularly to the *diakon* word group, and the conclusions reached for this study were that God’s grace is reflected in the *diakonia* of God’s sphere – humbly yet efficaciously – and that *diakonia* is also a corresponding ministry image of the nature and characteristics of the believing disciple.

In the final phase of their volume, Andreas Dohnke and Johannes Sailer seek to provide a practical-theoretical contribution. From their perspective, children that are engaged in active service and obedience to Christ are in a better position to become like Christ in their roles as representatives of Him before the world. The authors conclude that with a view to children’s *diakonia* and through children’s *diakonia*, parents and the church can promote the children’s spiritual development and see to it that children are not mere add-ons to the church, but real participants in the *Missio Dei*.
